title: Find upper bound of target
description: Find the first element strictly greater than the target in a sorted array

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1>4? No. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3, 3>4? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=5, 5>4? Yes. Return index 2.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=2, 2>9? No. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=4, 4>9? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=6, 6>9? No. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=8, 8>9? No. Loop ends, return length 4.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the sorted array from left to right, checking each element until finding the first one strictly greater than the target. Use this for small arrays or when simplicity is prioritized over speed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Upper Bound with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution: Find first element strictly greater than target in sorted array using linear search.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the upper bound leverages the sorted nature of the array to perform a simple linear scan. Intuition: Since the array is sorted in non-decreasing order, once we encounter an element greater than the target, all subsequent elements will also be greater (due to sorted property), making this the first such element. We iterate from left to right, checking each element against the target. The moment we find an element > target, we return its index. If no such element exists after checking all elements, we return a sentinel value (like array length) indicating the target would be inserted at the end. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize index i = 0. 2. While i < n (array length): if arr[i] > target, return i. Else, increment i. 3. If loop completes without finding, return n. Time: O(n) worst-case (scan entire array). Space: O(1) (only index variable). Connection to L1: This directly implements the linear search subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty arrays (return 0), not using strict greater than (using >= instead of >), or not returning sentinel when target exceeds all elements. Real pattern: def upper_bound(arr, target): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] > target: return i return len(arr)

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1>4? No. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3, 3>4? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=5, 5>4? Yes. Return index 2.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=2, 2>9? No. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=4, 4>9? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=6, 6>9? No. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=8, 8>9? No. Loop ends, return length 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Search for First Greater Element
ladders.desc: Find first element greater than target in any array (not necessarily sorted).
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs a linear search to locate the first occurrence of an element satisfying a condition (greater than target). Intuition: By scanning sequentially from the beginning, we guarantee finding the first matching element because we stop at the earliest index where condition holds. For sorted arrays, this efficiently finds upper bound; for unsorted, it finds first greater element but not necessarily upper bound in sorted sense. Step-by-step: 1. Loop through array indices 0 to n-1. 2. At each index i, check if arr[i] > target. 3. If true, return i immediately. 4. If loop completes, return n (not found). Time: O(n) worst-case. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is the core search mechanism used in the full problem. Common mistakes: Continuing search after finding match (should return immediately), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, incorrect sentinel value. Real pattern: def linear_search_greater(arr, target): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] > target: return i return len(arr)

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=3, 3>2? Yes. Return index 0 immediately.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1>2? No. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=1, 1>2? No. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=1, 1>2? No. Loop ends, return length 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison with Early Exit
ladders.desc: Iterate through elements, compare each to target, exit when condition met.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves traversing a sequence while performing comparisons and terminating early upon meeting a condition. Intuition: Many search problems require examining elements in order until a specific criterion is satisfied; once found, further processing is unnecessary. This early exit optimizes average-case performance. For upper bound, the condition is arr[i] > target. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize counter i = 0. 2. While i < n: perform comparison arr[i] > target. 3. If true, break loop and return i. 4. If false, increment i and continue. 5. After loop, handle not-found case. Time: O(n) worst-case, but O(k) average where k is position of first greater element. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented within the linear search subroutine. Common mistakes: Not breaking loop (continues to end), incorrect loop condition (i <= n causing index error), mishandling empty input. Real pattern: i = 0; while i < len(arr): if arr[i] > target: break; i += 1; return i

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10, 10>25? No, i=1. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20, 20>25? No, i=2. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=30, 30>25? Yes, break. Return i=2.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5, 5>5? No, i=1. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=5, 5>5? No, i=2. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=5, 5>5? No, i=3. Loop ends (i=3 not <3), return i=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterate through array elements while maintaining current index.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves sequentially accessing each element in an array using an index variable. Intuition: Arrays store elements contiguously in memory, allowing efficient access via indices. By incrementing an index from 0 to n-1, we can visit each element exactly once. This is fundamental for linear algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize index i = 0. 2. While i is less than array length n: access arr[i], perform some operation, then increment i by 1. 3. Loop terminates when i equals n. Time: O(n) for visiting n elements. Space: O(1) for index variable. Connection to L2: This traversal provides the mechanism to apply comparisons sequentially. Common mistakes: Starting index at 1 (missing first element), using i <= n (causes index out of bounds), not updating index (infinite loop). Real pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): # access arr[i]

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, access arr[0]=10. Step 2: i=1, access arr[1]=20. Step 3: i=2, access arr[2]=30. Step 4: i=3, loop ends.
Step 1: i=0, access arr[0]=5. Step 2: i=1, loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison (Greater Than)
ladders.desc: Compare two values to determine if one is strictly greater than the other.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves evaluating the relationship between two values using the greater-than operator (>). Intuition: Comparisons are fundamental in programming for decision-making; here, we check if an array element exceeds a target value. The operation returns a boolean (true/false). For numbers, it compares magnitudes; for other types, it uses defined ordering (e.g., lexicographic for strings). Step-by-step: Given values a and b, evaluate expression a > b. If true, a is greater than b; if false, a is less than or equal to b. Time: O(1) constant time. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: During array traversal, we apply this comparison to each element against the target. Common mistakes: Using >= (greater or equal) instead of > (strictly greater), comparing incompatible types, misunderstanding operator precedence. Real pattern: if element > target: # do something

examples.trace: Evaluate 7 > 3: 7 is greater than 3, so result is True.
Evaluate 5 > 5: 5 is not strictly greater than 5 (they are equal), so result is False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using zero-based indices.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an index. Intuition: Think of an array like a row of lockers numbered starting from 0; to get an item, you need its locker number (index). Zero-based indexing means the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. This is efficient because the memory address of element i can be computed directly from base address. Step-by-step: Given array arr and index i, access element with arr[i]. Ensure i is within valid range [0, length-1] to avoid errors. For example, arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for storing n elements. Connection to L4: Comparisons require accessing array elements via indices. Common mistakes: Using index 1 for first element (should be 0), accessing index equal to length (out of bounds), confusing index with value. Real pattern: element = arr[index]

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 1 corresponds to second element, which is 10.
Array has single element at index 0, which is 100.

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search to efficiently locate the upper bound by repeatedly halving the search interval based on comparisons with the target. This is optimal for sorted arrays, reducing search time significantly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Upper Bound Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find first element strictly greater than target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: The upper bound problem requires finding the smallest index where array[index] > target in a sorted array. Binary search is optimal because we can repeatedly halve the search space. The intuition: maintain two pointers (low, high) representing the current search interval. At each step, calculate mid = (low + high) // 2. If arr[mid] <= target, the upper bound must be to the right (low = mid + 1). If arr[mid] > target, this could be the answer, but there might be smaller indices to the left (high = mid - 1). We track the best candidate found so far. The algorithm: initialize low=0, high=n-1, result=n (default if no element > target). While low <= high: calculate mid; if arr[mid] > target: update result=mid, search left (high=mid-1); else: search right (low=mid+1). Return result. Time: O(log n) from halving search space. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the binary search subroutine directly. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty arrays, off-by-one errors in indices, not updating result correctly when arr[mid] > target. Real pattern: while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] > target: result=mid; high=mid-1; else: low=mid+1.

examples.trace: Initial: low=0, high=4, result=5. Step1: mid=2, arr[2]=5>4 → result=2, high=1. Step2: low=0, high=1, mid=0, arr[0]=1<=4 → low=1. Step3: low=1, high=1, mid=1, arr[1]=3<=4 → low=2. Loop ends (low>high). Return result=2.

Initial: low=0, high=3, result=4. Step1: mid=1, arr[1]=4<=9 → low=2. Step2: low=2, high=3, mid=2, arr[2]=6<=9 → low=3. Step3: low=3, high=3, mid=3, arr[3]=8<=9 → low=4. Loop ends. Return result=4 (no element > target).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Upper Bound Condition
ladders.desc: Implement binary search that finds first index where condition arr[mid] > target is true.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the upper bound solution. The intuition: we need to find the smallest index satisfying arr[index] > target. Binary search works because the array is sorted, so if arr[mid] > target, all elements to the right also > target (since sorted ascending), but we need the leftmost such index. Algorithm: initialize low=0, high=n-1, result=n. While low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] > target: result=mid, high=mid-1 (search left for smaller index); else: low=mid+1 (condition false, search right). Return result. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is exactly the main algorithm without problem-specific wrapper. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of adjusting search interval based on comparison. Common mistakes: using < instead of > in condition, not updating result when condition true, infinite loop if not updating low/high correctly. Real pattern: while low <= high: mid=(low+high)//2; if arr[mid] > target: result=mid; high=mid-1; else: low=mid+1.

examples.trace: Initial: low=0, high=3, result=4. Step1: mid=1, arr[1]=3<=4 → low=2. Step2: low=2, high=3, mid=2, arr[2]=5>4 → result=2, high=1. Loop ends (low>high). Return 2.
Initial: low=0, high=2, result=3. Step1: mid=1, arr[1]=4<=6 → low=2. Step2: low=2, high=2, mid=2, arr[2]=6<=6 → low=3. Loop ends. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Halving Search Interval with Comparison
ladders.desc: Practice adjusting low/high pointers in binary search based on arr[mid] comparison with target.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision logic in binary search. The intuition: at each step, we compare arr[mid] with target to decide which half to search next. Since array is sorted ascending: if arr[mid] <= target, the upper bound must be to the right (all elements left are <= target), so set low=mid+1. If arr[mid] > target, this could be the answer, but there might be smaller indices to the left, so set high=mid-1 to search left. We don't return immediately when arr[mid] > target because we need the first (smallest index) such element. Algorithm: given low, high, arr, target: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] <= target: low=mid+1; else: high=mid-1. Continue until low > high. Time: O(log n) per step. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the condition check and pointer update inside the loop. Connection to L3: This uses the mid calculation and comparison operations. Common mistakes: using < instead of <=, updating wrong pointer, not handling equality correctly (upper bound needs >, not >=). Real pattern: if arr[mid] <= target: low=mid+1; else: high=mid-1.

examples.trace: Step1: mid=1, arr[1]=3<=4 → low=2, high=3. Step2: mid=2, arr[2]=5>4 → low=2, high=1. Loop ends (low>high).
Step1: mid=1, arr[1]=4<=6 → low=2, high=3. Step2: mid=2, arr[2]=6<=6 → low=3, high=3. Step3: mid=3, arr[3]=8>6 → low=3, high=2. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculate Mid Index and Compare Elements
ladders.desc: Compute mid-point of interval and compare array element with target value.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two operations: calculating the middle index and performing a comparison. The intuition: binary search requires splitting the search interval, which is done by finding the mid index: mid = (low + high) // 2 (integer division). Then we compare arr[mid] with target to decide the next step. The comparison arr[mid] > target checks if the middle element is strictly greater than target, which is the condition for upper bound. Why integer division? It ensures mid is an integer index. Why compare? It tells us which half of the array to search next. Algorithm: given low, high, arr, target: mid = (low + high) // 2; check if arr[mid] > target. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the computation inside each loop iteration. Connection to L4: This uses basic arithmetic and comparison operations. Common mistakes: using floating-point division, off-by-one in mid calculation (e.g., (low+high)/2 without integer division), incorrect comparison operator. Real pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2; condition = arr[mid] > target.

examples.trace: Calculate mid: (0+5)//2=2. Access arr[2]=3. Compare: 3 > 3? False.
Calculate mid: (2+4)//2=3. Access arr[3]=4. Compare: 4 > 3? True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Integer Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and perform integer addition/division.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two skills: accessing an array element at a specific index and computing the midpoint using integer arithmetic. The intuition: arrays are zero-indexed, so arr[i] gives the element at position i. For binary search, we need to compute the middle index between low and high: mid = (low + high) // 2. The addition sums the bounds, and integer division (//) truncates to the nearest integer toward zero, ensuring mid is a valid index. Why integer division? Because array indices must be integers. Why addition? To find the center point. Algorithm: given low, high, arr: sum = low + high; mid = sum // 2; element = arr[mid]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is how we get mid and arr[mid] for comparison. Connection to L5: This builds on basic array and arithmetic concepts. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing, floating-point division resulting in float, not handling empty arrays. Real pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2; value = arr[mid].

examples.trace: Calculate sum: 0+4=4. Integer division: 4//2=2. Access arr[2]: value=30.
Calculate sum: 1+3=4. Integer division: 4//2=2. Access arr[2]: value=30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sorted Arrays and Greater-Than Comparison
ladders.desc: Understand that arrays are sorted ascending and what 'greater than' means.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding that an array is sorted in non-decreasing order (ascending), meaning each element is less than or equal to the next one. Also, understanding the comparison 'greater than' (>): for two numbers a and b, a > b is true if a is larger than b. The intuition: if an array is sorted, then for any index i, all elements at indices < i are <= arr[i], and all elements at indices > i are >= arr[i]. This property allows binary search to work. The 'greater than' condition is used to find the upper bound. Why sorted? Because binary search relies on order to eliminate half the array. Why >? Because upper bound requires strictly greater, not greater or equal. Algorithm: given arr, check if it's sorted: for i from 0 to n-2, ensure arr[i] <= arr[i+1]. Given target and value, check if value > target. Time: O(1) for comparison, O(n) to verify sortedness. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This is the context in which array indexing and comparisons are used. Common mistakes: confusing sorted ascending vs descending, misunderstanding > vs >=. Real pattern: is_sorted = all(arr[i] <= arr[i+1] for i in range(len(arr)-1)); is_greater = value > target.

examples.input: arr=[1,3,5,7], check if sorted and if 5 > 3
examples.trace: Check sorted: 1<=3 True, 3<=5 True, 5<=7 True → sorted. Check 5>3: 5 is larger than 3 → True.

examples.input: arr=[2,2,1,4], check if sorted and if 2 > 2
examples.trace: Check sorted: 2<=2 True, 2<=1 False → not sorted. Check 2>2: 2 is not larger than 2 → False.

approaches.name: Recursive Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Implement binary search recursively by dividing the array and making recursive calls to find the upper bound. Suitable for educational contexts or when recursion is preferred for clarity.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(log n) due to recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Binary Search for Upper Bound
ladders.desc: Complete recursive implementation to find first element strictly greater than target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the upper bound (first element strictly greater than target) in a sorted array using recursive binary search. The intuition leverages the sorted property: if arr[mid] <= target, the upper bound must be in the right half (mid+1 to high); otherwise, it could be at mid or in the left half. We maintain a result variable that tracks the best candidate found so far. The algorithm recursively divides the search space, updating result when arr[mid] > target. Base cases handle empty ranges. Time complexity is O(log n) due to halving search space each recursion. Space complexity is O(log n) for recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the recursive helper as the complete solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, incorrect base cases, and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: define recursive function with parameters (arr, target, low, high, result), compute mid, compare arr[mid] to target, make recursive calls with updated bounds, return result.

examples.trace: Initial: low=0, high=4, result=-1. Step 1: mid=2, arr[2]=5 > 4, update result=5, recurse left: low=0, high=1. Step 2: mid=0, arr[0]=1 <= 4, recurse right: low=1, high=1. Step 3: mid=1, arr[1]=3 <= 4, recurse right: low=2, high=1 (empty), return result=5.

Initial: low=0, high=3, result=-1. Step 1: mid=1, arr[1]=4 <= 9, recurse right: low=2, high=3. Step 2: mid=2, arr[2]=6 <= 9, recurse right: low=3, high=3. Step 3: mid=3, arr[3]=8 <= 9, recurse right: low=4, high=3 (empty), return result=-1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Binary Search Helper Function
ladders.desc: Recursive function that searches for upper bound within given bounds.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recursive logic for finding the upper bound within a specific range [low, high]. The intuition is to recursively narrow down the search space by comparing the middle element to the target. If arr[mid] <= target, the upper bound must be to the right; if arr[mid] > target, mid is a candidate, and we search left for potentially smaller candidates. We pass a result parameter to track the best candidate found. The algorithm: if low > high, return result (base case). Compute mid = (low + high) // 2. If arr[mid] > target, update result = arr[mid], recursively search left (low, mid-1). Else, recursively search right (mid+1, high). Time: O(log n) as range halves each call. Space: O(log n) for stack. This feeds into L0 by being called with full array bounds. Mistakes: forgetting to update result, incorrect recursion parameters. Code pattern: def recursive_upper_bound(arr, target, low, high, result).

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=3 > 2, result=3, recurse left: low=0, high=0. Step 2: mid=0, arr[0]=1 <= 2, recurse right: low=1, high=0 (empty), return result=3.
Step 1: mid=1, arr[1]=4 <= 7, recurse right: low=2, high=2. Step 2: mid=2, arr[2]=6 <= 7, recurse right: low=3, high=2 (empty), return result=-1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide and Conquer with Midpoint Comparison
ladders.desc: Technique of comparing target to middle element to decide search direction.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves dividing the array at the midpoint and using the comparison with the target to determine which half to search next. The intuition: in a sorted array, if arr[mid] <= target, all elements up to mid are <= target, so the upper bound must be in the right half (mid+1 to high). If arr[mid] > target, mid could be the upper bound, but there might be a smaller element > target in the left half, so we search left. This halving ensures O(log n) time. The algorithm: compute mid index, compare arr[mid] to target, update search bounds accordingly. This feeds into L1 by being the decision step in recursion. Mistakes: using < instead of <=, incorrect bound updates. Code pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2; if arr[mid] <= target: search right; else: search left.

examples.trace: mid = 1, arr[1] = 20 <= 25, so upper bound must be in right half (indices 2 to 3).
mid = 1, arr[1] = 20 <= 35, search right: low=2, high=3. New mid=2, arr[2]=30 <= 35, search right: low=3, high=3. Mid=3, arr[3]=40 > 35, so search left (but empty), result=40.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function Calls with Parameters
ladders.desc: Implementing recursion with parameters to track search state.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves making recursive function calls that pass updated parameters to narrow the search space. The intuition is that each recursive call should work on a smaller subarray, so we pass low and high indices to define the current range. Additionally, we pass a result parameter to accumulate the best candidate found. The algorithm: define a function that takes arr, target, low, high, result. In the recursive call, update low/high based on comparison, and possibly update result. Base case stops when low > high. This feeds into L2 by enabling the divide-and-conquer logic. Mistakes: not passing updated result, infinite recursion due to incorrect bounds. Code pattern: def recurse(arr, target, low, high, result): if low > high: return result; mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] > target: return recurse(arr, target, low, mid-1, arr[mid]); else: return recurse(arr, target, mid+1, high, result).

examples.input: Initial call: arr=[1,3,5], target=2, low=0, high=2, result=-1
examples.trace: Call 1: low=0, high=2, mid=1, arr[1]=3>2, update result=3, call 2: low=0, high=0. Call 2: mid=0, arr[0]=1<=2, call 3: low=1, high=0. Call 3: base case, return result=3.

examples.input: Initial call: arr=[2,4], target=5, low=0, high=1, result=-1
examples.trace: Call 1: mid=0, arr[0]=2<=5, call 2: low=1, high=1. Call 2: mid=1, arr[1]=4<=5, call 3: low=2, high=1. Call 3: base case, return result=-1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Midpoint Calculation
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and computing middle index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing elements in an array by index and calculating the midpoint of a range. The intuition is that arrays allow O(1) access to any element using its index, and the midpoint is used to divide the search space in binary search. The algorithm: given low and high indices, compute mid = (low + high) // 2 using integer division to get the floor value. Then access arr[mid] to compare with target. This feeds into L3 by providing the data needed for recursive decisions. Mistakes: off-by-one in mid calculation (e.g., using ceil), accessing out-of-bounds indices. Code pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2; element = arr[mid].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Basic idea of sorted order and comparing numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding that a sorted array arr has elements in non-decreasing order (arr[i] <= arr[i+1] for all i). This property allows efficient search because comparing a target to one element tells us about others. For example, if target < arr[mid], then target must be less than all elements to the right of mid due to sorted order. The intuition is that order enables elimination of large portions of the array. This feeds into L4 by justifying why midpoint comparison works. Mistakes: assuming array is strictly increasing (can have duplicates), not leveraging sorted property. Code pattern: checking if arr is sorted, comparing two numbers.

examples.output: Since 3 < 4 < 5, and array sorted, upper bound is 5.
examples.trace: Array sorted: 1 <= 3 <= 5 <= 7. Compare target 4 to elements: 3 < 4, 5 > 4, so first >4 is 5.
Sorted with duplicates: 2 <= 2 <= 4 <= 4. Target 2: elements <=2 are at indices 0,1; first >2 is 4 at index 2.
